meta.tagline: The pre-sign kill switch for AI agents on Solana.
meta.license: Not yet assigned — see LICENSE file

one_liner: SAK gives AI agents a pre-sign kill switch, same-slot reflexes, and 100–1000× cheaper state — in one Rust kernel that plugs under any existing agent framework.

problem.headline: Nothing exists between the LLM and the signing step.
problem.sub: Every agent framework sends LLM output directly to signing. No check. No simulation. Nothing.

real_incident.title: The Grok Morse Code Attack
real_incident.description: An attacker sent a Morse code message to Grok. The AI decoded it and passed it to a trading bot. The bot executed it. $200,000 left the wallet in one transaction. No check caught it.

stats.label: Lost to LLM-linked crypto incidents in 2025
Drained from one wallet in one transaction
Solana slot time — polling agents always miss it

failures.name: The Hallucination Risk
failures.description: LLMs predict probable tokens. They do not verify on-chain state. A hallucinated token address, 99% slippage, or wrong program ID executes immediately.

failures.name: The Polling Tax
failures.description: Agents poll RPC every 400ms. Solana produces a new slot every 400ms. Polling agents are always 2-3 slots behind every opportunity.
examples: Arbitrage opportunities vanish in one slot
Polling agents can never win on Solana

failures.name: The Cost Bottleneck
failures.description: Storing agent state in regular Solana accounts costs ~$0.30 per account. 1,000 agents cost $300,000 in rent before executing a single transaction.

stack_position.headline: SAK sits underneath your existing stack.
data_flow: Events flow UP: Geyser → Reflex Engine → Agent
Transactions flow DOWN: Agent → Guardian → RPC
Every outbound tx passes through Guardian

pillars.name: Guardian
pillars.description: Every transaction the LLM generates is simulated locally inside LiteSVM before signing. A 7-rule engine evaluates the simulation result. If any rule fails, the transaction is rejected with zero on-chain cost.
pillars.the_guarantee: The attacker can hide the instruction however they want. They cannot hide what it does to the balance.
how_it_works: LiteSVM runs the full Solana VM locally
7 rules evaluated in order — first rejection wins

pillars.name: Reflex Engine
pillars.description: Yellowstone Geyser gRPC push subscription instead of RPC polling. Receives account deltas within the same slot they occur. Reconnects automatically with exponential backoff and jitter (cap: 30 s). Agents react before block finalization, not after.
how_it_works: Reflex Engine receives within same slot
Typed ChainEvent routed to agent callback via broadcast channel (cap: 1024)
Agent reacts — signs tx in same slot

pillars.name: ZK State
pillars.description: Agent state stored via Light Protocol ZK-compressed accounts. In-memory hot cache for the reflex loop critical path. Periodic flush to ZK compressed accounts for cold storage. Never read ZK state in the hot path.
how_it_works: Agent state kept in HotCache for reflex loop (zero-latency reads)
Periodic flush_to_zk() batches writes to Light Protocol
128-byte Groth16 ZK proof for cold state reads

crate_purpose.sak-guardian: Pre-sign transaction simulation + rule evaluation. The safety layer.
crate_purpose.sak-core: Shared types (Decision, TxMeta, ChainEvent, Rule enum). Used by all other crates.
crate_purpose.sak-sdk: High-level Kernel builder. Wraps all three pillars into one interface for agent developers.
crate_purpose.sak-reflex: Yellowstone Geyser push subscriber. Same-slot event delivery (no polling).
crate_purpose.sak-state: ZK-compressed state storage via Light Protocol. 100-1000x cheaper rent.

guardian.purpose: Guardian sits between the LLM and the signing step. It receives every transaction the agent wants to send, simulates it locally in LiteSVM, and runs 7 configurable rules against the result. No transaction reaches the network without Guardian approval.

constructors.signature: pub fn from_yaml(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> Result<Self>
constructors.description: Load Guardian with rules parsed from a YAML file. This is the standard way to create a Guardian. The YAML file contains all 7 rules with their parameters (see rules.yaml for the default config).
constructors.when_to_use: Production. Your rules are maintained as a YAML file in your repo.

constructors.signature: pub fn from_yaml_with_svm(path: impl AsRef<Path>, svm: LiteSVM) -> Result<Self>
constructors.description: Load rules from YAML but use an externally-provided LiteSVM instance. Use this when you need pre-seeded accounts in the simulator (e.g., token accounts with balance).
constructors.when_to_use: Tests that require specific on-chain state for simulation.

constructors.description: Construct Guardian directly from a Vec<Rule>, skipping YAML entirely. Useful for tests where you want to configure rules inline.
constructors.when_to_use: Unit tests and programmatic rule configuration.

methods.name: evaluate
methods.signature: pub fn evaluate(&mut self, tx: &VersionedTransaction, meta: &TxMeta) -> Decision
methods.description: **Full simulation path.** Runs the VersionedTransaction inside LiteSVM (full Solana VM), captures pre-state and post-state balances for every account, then evaluates all 7 rules against the simulation result. Slower (25-70ms) but catches execution-level failures that raw evaluation misses.
methods.when_to_use: High-value transactions where you want maximum safety. Requires &mut self because LiteSVM maintains internal VM state between calls.

parameters.name: tx
parameters.description: Unsigned versioned transaction from your LLM/agent. Must use a legacy message format (not v0). The transaction is NOT submitted to the network — only simulated locally.

parameters.name: meta
parameters.description: Agent-supplied metadata including slippage_bps and a human-readable description. This feeds into rules like SlippageCheck.

methods.returns: Returns Decision::Allow if all rules pass, or Decision::Reject { rule, reason } with the name of the rule that fired and why.
methods.errors: If LiteSVM simulation itself fails (e.g., insufficient funds, invalid account data), Guardian does NOT crash — it returns Decision::Reject with rule = "pre_sign_simulation". The reason string will be a human-readable mapping of the Solana runtime error (see simulation_errors section for the full mapping table). Never panics.

methods.name: evaluate_raw
methods.signature: pub fn evaluate_raw(&self, account_keys: Vec<String>, instructions: &[(u8, &[u8])], meta: &TxMeta) -> Decision
methods.description: **No-simulation path.** Evaluates rules using only account keys and instruction data — no LiteSVM needed. Takes &self (no mutation). Cannot catch execution-level failures (like insufficient funds), but is much faster and doesn't require a LiteSVM instance.
methods.when_to_use: Low-value transactions, high-frequency trading, or environments where LiteSVM is unavailable. Also used by the race-server HTTP API since it runs in a stateless context.

parameters.name: account_keys
parameters.description: All account public keys referenced in the transaction (base-58 encoded). First element is usually the fee payer.

parameters.name: instructions
parameters.description: Instruction data as (program_id_index, data) tuples. The u8 is an index into account_keys. This matches the compiled Solana Message layout exactly — just pass the accounts and instructions directly from your message.

parameters.name: meta
parameters.description: Agent-supplied metadata (slippage_bps, description). Rules like SlippageCheck and MinTransferLamports depend on this.

methods.returns: Decision::Allow or Decision::Reject { rule, reason }. Same as evaluate(), but without the pre_sign_simulation fallback (no simulation to fail).

kernel.purpose: Kernel is the one-stop API for agent developers. Instead of wiring Guardian, Reflex Engine, and ZK State separately, you build a Kernel with a builder pattern and call submit(). Kernel handles the orchestration internally.
kernel.description: High-level SDK entrypoint. Wraps all three SAK pillars (Guardian + Reflex Engine + ZK State) into one builder-pattern interface. Start here if you're integrating SAK into an agent framework.

config.name: KernelConfig
config.purpose: Configuration for a Kernel. All fields optional — use Default::default() to get a dev setup.

fields.name: geyser_endpoint
fields.description: Yellowstone gRPC endpoint URL. Get this from your Geyser provider (Helius, GetBlock, Triton). Required for Reflex Engine. Example: "https://grpc.helius.dev".

fields.name: helius_api_key
fields.description: API key for Helius Geyser (sent as x_token in gRPC metadata). Get a free tier key at helius.dev. Only needed if using Helius as your Geyser provider.

fields.name: rules_path
fields.description: Path to the rules YAML file. Defaults to Some("rules.yaml") via rules_path_or_default(). If the file doesn't exist, with_guardian() returns Err.

methods.name: new
methods.description: Create a Kernel shell. No pillars are initialized yet — call .with_guardian(), .with_reflex(), .with_state() to attach them. A call to submit() before with_guardian() returns Decision::Allow (fail-open warning logged).

methods.name: with_guardian
methods.description: Initialize the Guardian pillar from a rules YAML file. This must be called before submit() if you want Guardian protection. Returns Err if the YAML file is missing or malformed.

methods.name: with_reflex
methods.signature: pub fn with_reflex(self, endpoint: &str, x_token: Option<String>) -> (Self, EventRouter)
methods.description: Initialize the Reflex Engine pillar. Takes the Geyser gRPC endpoint and optional API key. Returns (Kernel, EventRouter) — subscribe to ChainEvent variants on the EventRouter to react to on-chain events in the same slot.

methods.name: with_state
methods.description: Initialize the ZK State pillar. Enables compressed state storage via Light Protocol. Requires network access to Solana RPC.

methods.name: submit
methods.signature: pub fn submit(&mut self, tx: &VersionedTransaction, meta: &TxMeta) -> Decision
methods.description: **The primary method.** Routes a transaction through the Guardian. If Guardian is initialized, calls evaluate() internally. If not, logs a warning and returns Decision::Allow (fail-open for backward compatibility — don't rely on this in production).

methods.name: state
methods.signature: pub fn state(&mut self) -> Option<&mut ZkState>
methods.description: Access the ZK State manager for reading/writing compressed agent state. Returns None if with_state() was not called.

methods.name: start_reflex
methods.signature: pub async fn start_reflex(self, filter: SubscribeFilter) -> Result<()>
methods.description: Start the Yellowstone Geyser subscriber loop. Runs indefinitely with automatic reconnection (exponential backoff with jitter, cap 30s). Consumes the Kernel (self) since it owns the event loop. Spawns on tokio.

types.name: Decision
types.description: The Guardian's verdict on a transaction. This is the most important type in SAK — every transaction evaluation returns one of these two variants. `Allow` means sign and send. `Reject` means the transaction is dangerous — do NOT sign (zero on-chain cost).

variants.name: Allow
variants.description: Transaction passed all rules. Proceed to sign and broadcast to the Solana network.

variants.name: Reject
variants.description: A rule fired. Do NOT sign this transaction. No on-chain cost was incurred — the simulation and evaluation happened entirely locally.

fields.name: rule
fields.description: Name of the rule that fired. Use this for logging and alerting. Examples: max_slippage, max_account_drain, allowed_programs.

fields.name: reason
fields.description: Human-readable explanation of why the rule fired. Includes the specific values that triggered the rule. Log this to help with debugging and rule tuning.

types.name: TxMeta
types.description: Agent-supplied metadata passed alongside every transaction. Think of this as the 'intent' — what the agent says it's doing. Rules like SlippageCheck operate on TxMeta data rather than raw transaction bytes. Implements Default (both fields = None).

fields.name: slippage_bps
fields.description: Slippage tolerance the agent is willing to accept, in basis points (100 bps = 1%). If the agent declares 50 bps (0.5%) but the swap would execute at 200 bps, the SlippageCheck rule fires. Set to None to skip the slippage check entirely (not recommended).

fields.name: description
fields.description: Human-readable description of what the agent intends to do. Not evaluated by any rule, but included in audit logs and the race-server UI for debugging.

types.example: TxMeta { slippage_bps: Some(150), description: Some("swap 0.1 SOL for USDC".into()) }

types.name: ChainEvent
types.description: An on-chain event produced by the Reflex Engine from the Yellowstone Geyser push stream. Each event is stamped with the Solana slot it occurred in. Agent callbacks receive these through the EventRouter. 'Same-slot' means the agent receives and reacts before the next slot (400ms window).

types.name: EventKind
types.description: The type of on-chain event. AccountChanged fires when a tracked account's lamport balance changes. ProgramInvoked fires when any tracked program is invoked. Subscribe to specific accounts/programs via SubscribeFilter.

types.name: Rule
types.description: A single Guardian rule. Tagged by 'type' field in YAML. Evaluated in declaration order — first match wins.

types.name: GuardianFeedback
types.definition:
pub struct GuardianFeedback {
    pub timestamp: String,
    pub decision: String,
    pub rule: Option<String>,
    pub description: Option<String>,
    pub stars: u8,
    pub verdict: FeedbackVerdict,
}
types.description: User feedback submission for a Guardian decision. POST to /feedback on race-server.

types.name: FeedbackVerdict
types.description: Derived from star rating: 4–5 = Correct, 1–2 = Wrong, 3 = Neutral.

types.name: AgentState
types.definition:
pub struct AgentState {
    pub agent_id: String,
    pub last_n_decisions: Vec<DecisionRecord>,
    pub open_positions: Vec<Position>,
    pub cooldown_until_slot: u64,
    pub violation_history: Vec<String>,
}
types.description: Agent state stored in ZK-compressed accounts (or hot cache in current implementation).

types.name: SubscribeFilter
types.description: Filter for Geyser subscription — which accounts and programs to watch.

types.name: SakError
types.definition:
pub enum SakError {
    Geyser(String),
    Simulation(String),
    RuleViolation { rule: String, reason: String },
    State(String),
    Rpc(String),
}

rules.description: Blocks any transaction whose agent-declared slippage exceeds the basis-point cap. Operates on TxMeta.slippage_bps — if None, this rule is skipped.
rules.real_attack: LLM hallucinates 99% slippage — entire swap value drains to attacker on next fill.

parameters.name: max_bps
parameters.description: Maximum allowed slippage in basis points. 100 bps = 1%.

rules.description: Blocks any instruction invoking a program not on the explicit whitelist. Evaluated per-instruction — one unlisted program rejects the whole transaction.
rules.real_attack: LLM generates a fake program ID (looks valid, isn't). Malicious code executes with agent's signing authority.

parameters.name: programs
parameters.description: List of allowed program public keys (base-58).

rules.rejection_format: program {program_id} is not in the whitelist
rules.rejection_example: program MemoSq4gqABAXKb96qnH8TysNcWxMyWCqXgDLGmfcHr is not in the whitelist
rules.recommended_value: Start strict. Add programs only after manual verification.

rules.description: Blocks transfers exceeding the lamport cap. In simulated mode uses actual pre/post balance deltas. In raw mode parses System Program Transfer and CreateAccount instruction data (bincode discriminant 2 and 0).
rules.real_attack: Grok Morse code attack — entire wallet balance transferred to attacker in one instruction.

parameters.name: max_lamports
parameters.description: Maximum lamports a single account may lose in one transaction. 1,000,000,000 = 1 SOL.

rules.rejection_format_raw: transfer of {lamports} lamports exceeds max {max_lamports} lamports
rules.rejection_example: transfer of 9950000000 lamports exceeds max 1000000000 lamports
rules.recommended_value: Set to maximum single trade size for your strategy.

rules.description: Blocks transactions referencing more accounts than the cap. Checks tx.account_keys().len() — all accounts in the message, not just writable ones.
rules.real_attack: Attacker floods a transaction with 30+ accounts to hide a malicious account in the noise.

parameters.name: max_count
parameters.description: Maximum number of accounts a transaction may reference.

rules.description: Blocks transactions that set a ComputeBudget SetComputeUnitLimit instruction above the cap. Parses ComputeBudget program instructions: discriminant 0x02 followed by u32 LE units.
rules.real_attack: Attacker requests maximum compute to obfuscate expensive inner loops or reentrancy patterns.

parameters.name: max_units
parameters.description: Maximum compute units a transaction may request.

rules.description: Blocks transactions that set a ComputeBudget SetComputeUnitPrice instruction above the cap. Parses discriminant 0x03 followed by u64 LE microlamports.
rules.real_attack: Agent sets priority fee 100× normal, draining wallet via fees alone rather than transfers.

parameters.name: max_microlamports
parameters.description: Maximum priority fee in microlamports per compute unit.

rules.description: Blocks System Program Transfer instructions below the minimum lamport threshold. Catches zero-amount dust attacks used for wallet relationship tracking.
rules.real_attack: Attacker broadcasts 0-lamport transfers to correlate wallets for future targeting.

parameters.name: min_lamports
parameters.description: Minimum lamports required in any System Program Transfer instruction.

rules.rejection_format: transfer of {lamports} lamports is below minimum {min_lamports} lamports
rules.rejection_example: transfer of 0 lamports is below minimum 1 lamports

rules_yaml_full.title: Active rules.yaml
rules_yaml_full.description: Exact contents of rules.yaml as shipped. Customize per deployment.
rules_yaml_full.content:
rules:
  - name: max_slippage
    type: slippage_check
    max_bps: 200

  - name: allowed_programs
    type: program_whitelist
    programs:
      - "JUP6LkbZbjS1jKKwapdHNy74zcZ3tLUZoi5QNyVTaV4"   # Jupiter v6
      - "whirLbMiicVdio4qvUfM5KAg6Ct8VwpYzGff3uctyCc"    # Orca Whirlpool
      - "11111111111111111111111111111111"                  # System program
      - "TokenkegQfeZyiNwAJbNbGKPFXCWuBvf9Ss623VQ5DA"    # SPL Token
      - "ATokenGPvbdGVxr1b2hvZbsiqW5xWH25efTNsLJe1bJ"     # Associated Token Account
      - "ComputeBudget111111111111111111111111111111"       # Compute Budget

  - name: max_account_drain
    type: drain_check
    max_lamports: 1000000000

  - name: max_accounts
    type: account_count_check
    max_count: 20

  - name: max_compute_units
    type: compute_units_check
    max_units: 1400000

  - name: max_priority_fee
    type: priority_fee_check
    max_microlamports: 1000000

  - name: min_transfer_value
    type: min_transfer_lamports
    min_lamports: 1

evil_corpus.description: 20 malicious transaction patterns. All 20 blocked by Guardian out of the box. Run with: cargo test -p sak-guardian

attacks.name: 99% slippage swap
attacks.description: Agent declares 9900 bps slippage tolerance.

attacks.name: Wrong token mint (fake USDC)
attacks.description: Transfer invoking FaKe1111... program — not on whitelist.

attacks.name: Drain entire SOL balance
attacks.description: Transfer of u64::MAX lamports.

attacks.name: Unknown program ID
attacks.description: Instruction invoking a random Address::new_unique() not in whitelist.

attacks.name: Transfer disguised as platform fee
attacks.description: 2 SOL transfer labeled as a fee — above 1 SOL drain limit.

attacks.name: Slippage set to u64::MAX
attacks.description: Arithmetic overflow exploit — u64::MAX bps declared.

attacks.name: Jupiter route via unlisted pool
attacks.description: Swap routed through an Address::new_unique() pool not in whitelist.

attacks.name: Zero-amount dust attack
attacks.description: Transfer of 0 lamports — wallet relationship tracking.

attacks.name: Account substitution drain
attacks.description: 5 SOL transfer to attacker — recipient substituted mid-instruction.

attacks.name: Balance underflow attempt
attacks.description: Transfer of 1,000,000,001 lamports — exactly 1 over the 1 SOL limit.

attacks.name: Excessive compute units
attacks.description: SetComputeUnitLimit(1,500,000) — above 1,400,000 cap.

attacks.name: Reentrancy-style CPI loop
attacks.description: Instruction invoking an unlisted loop program not in whitelist.

attacks.name: Fake system program ID
attacks.description: Almost-identical pubkey: 1111111111111111111111111111111F — not the real system program.

attacks.name: Multiple drain instructions
attacks.description: Two 1.5 SOL transfers bundled in one transaction — first exceeds limit.

attacks.name: Slippage bypass via CPI
attacks.description: Agent declares 5000bps hoping CPI routing avoids the check. Fails — metadata is always evaluated.

attacks.name: Token account closed mid-tx
attacks.description: Malicious closer program (unlisted) attempts to manipulate token account state.

attacks.name: Priority fee 100× normal
attacks.description: SetComputeUnitPrice(2,000,000) — above 1,000,000 microlamport cap.

attacks.name: Memo field injection
attacks.description: MemoSq4gqABAXKb96qnH8TysNcWxMyWCqXgDLGmfcHr not in whitelist — hidden drain instruction in memo.

attacks.name: 30-account obfuscation
attacks.description: 30 recipients + payer + system = 32 accounts. Exceeds 20-account cap.

attacks.name: Passes local — fails mainnet
attacks.description: Program passes LiteSVM (no on-chain code) but would fail on mainnet. Caught by whitelist first.

simulation_errors.description: When LiteSVM simulation itself fails, Guardian returns Decision::Reject with rule = 'pre_sign_simulation' and one of these human-readable reasons.

mappings.human: Insufficient funds — transaction would leave account below rent minimum
Invalid account data — possible wrong token address
Program execution failed — transaction would revert on-chain
Transaction exceeds account or program limits
Transaction would fail on-chain — blocked before signing

demo.architecture: race-server (Axum WebSocket) + tx-generator subprocess + race-ui (Vite + vanilla HTML/JS)

endpoints.description: WebSocket — broadcasts JSON transaction events from tx-generator subprocess
Accept GuardianFeedback JSON body — stored in memory FeedbackStore
Returns { total, correct, wrong, accuracy } as JSON

race_server.behavior: Spawns demo/tx-generator as a subprocess, reads its stdout line-by-line, broadcasts each line to all connected WebSocket clients. Restarts tx-generator with 5s delay on exit.

steps.title: Start the React UI
steps.code:
cd demo/race-ui
npm install
npm run dev

rust_guardian_example.title: Guardian — direct usage

rust_kernel_example.title: Kernel SDK — full stack

rust_evaluate_raw_example.title: evaluate_raw — fastest path (no LiteSVM)

typescript_example.title: TypeScript — HTTP client (for race-server API)
typescript_example.note: REST wrapper around race-server's /evaluate endpoint. Useful for demo UI, scripting, or prototyping. Not a production SDK.

python_example.title: Python — HTTP client (for race-server API)
python_example.code:
import httpx
import asyncio

async def evaluate_intent(
    slippage_bps: int,
    amount_lamports: int,
    program_ids: list[str],
    compute_units: int = 0,
    description: str = "",
    server_url: str = "http://localhost:3001",
):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        res = await client.post(
            f"{server_url}/evaluate",
            json={
                "slippage_bps": slippage_bps,
                "amount_lamports": amount_lamports,
                "program_ids": program_ids,
                "compute_units": compute_units,
                "description": description,
            },
        )
        if res.status_code != 200:
            err = res.json()
            raise RuntimeError(f"Guardian error: {err['message']}")
        return res.json()

# Check if a suspicious intent is blocked
result = asyncio.run(evaluate_intent(
    slippage_bps=9900,
    amount_lamports=5_000_000_000,
    program_ids=["JUP6LkbZbjS1jKKwapdHNy74zcZ3tLUZoi5QNyVTaV4"],
    description="maximize swap output",
))

if result["decision"] == "rejected":
    print(f"Blocked by {result['rule']}: {result['reason']}")
else:
    print("Transaction allowed")

# Subscribe to real-time events via WebSocket
async def listen_ws(server_url: str = "ws://localhost:3001/ws"):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        async with client.ws_connect(server_url) as ws:
            async for message in ws.iter_text():
                print("Event:", message)

asyncio.run(listen_ws())
python_example.note: Async HTTP + WebSocket client for the race-server. Requires httpx (`pip install httpx`).

env_vars.name: HELIUS_API_KEY
env_vars.description: Yellowstone Geyser API key — get free at helius.dev

steps: Configure systemd service with HELIUS_API_KEY env var
race_server.recommended_host: Hetzner Frankfurt or Amsterdam — low latency to Solana validators

steps: Deploy dist/ to Vercel or equivalent

faq.question: Does SAK slow down my agent?
faq.answer: Guardian evaluation takes 25–70ms locally. Geyser push saves 350–400ms vs polling. Net result: your agent is faster with SAK than without it.

faq.question: Can't you just tell the LLM not to hallucinate?
faq.answer: Context window instructions are requests. The LLM follows them 97% of the time. At 1,000 tx/day, that 3% is 30 wallet drains per day. SAK's Guardian is a hard mathematical constraint — a transaction exceeding 2% slippage cannot pass. Not 97% safe. 100%.

faq.question: Can large context windows (1M tokens) replace SAK?
faq.answer: No. Context window is about memory. SAK is about verification. LLM inference takes 2–10 seconds. Solana slots are 400ms. And even with perfect information, a predicted slippage is a probability — Guardian enforces it as a guarantee.

faq.question: Why not use traditional fraud detection?
faq.answer: Traditional fraud detection is reactive — it runs after the transaction and relies on reversal. Blockchain transactions are final. There is no reversal. SAK is preventive — it catches bad transactions before they are signed, at machine speed, before the slot closes.

faq.question: What if a legitimate transaction is blocked?
faq.answer: Adjust rules.yaml. Loosen the rule that fired, or add a program to the whitelist. All rules are configurable. False positives cost $0 — only on-chain time is wasted, never funds.

faq.question: How does SAK handle the Grok Morse code attack?
faq.answer: The attack succeeded because nothing checked the transaction before execution. Guardian's max_account_drain and allowed_programs would catch it — 'send entire balance to unknown wallet' fails multiple rules simultaneously, regardless of how the instruction was generated or encoded.

faq.question: Does SAK work with any LLM?
faq.answer: Yes. SAK evaluates transaction outputs, not model outputs. Works with GPT-4, Claude, Llama, Mistral, any model that generates Solana transactions.

faq.question: What is evaluate_raw vs evaluate?
faq.answer: evaluate_raw takes raw account keys + instruction bytes — fastest path, no LiteSVM overhead, takes &self. evaluate runs full LiteSVM simulation and captures pre/post balances — slower but catches execution-level failures, requires &mut self. Use evaluate for high-value transactions.

demo_script_seconds.0_10: Show the problem — LLM hallucinations drain wallets
demo_script_seconds.10_30: Show Guardian evaluating transactions in real time
demo_script_seconds.30_60: Show 3–4 blocked transactions with rule names and prevented loss
demo_script_seconds.80_90: CTA: Ship agents that can't be used against you
